Claude responded: I'll start by exploring what's in the zip, then figure out how to turn it into an interactive app.

Cataloging automotive data toolkit components

I'll start by exploring what's in the zip, then figure out how to turn it into an interactive app. Let me read the file-reading skill first since this is an uploaded archive.

Reading the file-reading skill to handle the uploaded archive correctly

Let me list the zip contents first.

Examined automotive data toolkit to architect interactive application

This is an ACES (automotive aftermarket data) parsing toolkit — right in your wheelhouse. Before I build anything, let me understand what these tools actually do. Let me read the README and the Python scripts.

Excavated Python tools from zip contents

Now let me read the actual tool scripts to understand the data pipeline.

Deciphering automotive data pipeline structure and output

Let me read the core processing tools to understand the transformations.

Mapping automotive aftermarket data pipeline architecture

Let me check the Buyers Guide and Parts Master tools, then look at what the actual output data looks like.

Weighing strategies for handling massive dataset

Now let me look at the actual output data — the Parts Master is the headline deliverable. Let me inspect it.

Weighing file size against interactive app feasibility

This is rich data — ~23,900 SKUs, each with US/Canada/Mexico VIO (vehicles-in-operation market size), weighted average vehicle age, year coverage, and the makes/models each part fits. The natural interactive app is a Parts Master Explorer: the synthesis deliverable of the whole pipeline.

Let me convert the real data to compact JSON and check its size so I know whether to embed it fully.

Architecting interactive app from zip contents

~24K SKUs, 16 part types, 2.8MB of real data — and the totals are striking (7.7B US vehicles-in-operation reach across the catalog). This is a real intelligence tool, not a toy. I'll build a self-contained, deployable Parts Master Explorer (single HTML file — portable, Netlify-ready, handles 24K rows with virtualization). Let me read the design skill first.

Architecting interactive HTML app with embedded data

I've got the full picture: this pipeline cross-references MPA's ACES fitment files against Experian VIO data to size each SKU's real-world market (how many vehicles it fits across US/Canada/Mexico), plus weighted vehicle age and year coverage. The Parts Master is the synthesis — that's what deserves to be interactive.

I'll build a Parts Master Explorer: a deployable single-file app over all ~24K real SKUs, with an instrument/spec-sheet aesthetic grounded in the automotive-catalog subject (oxblood as the brake-dust signal color, IBM Plex Mono for all the part numbers and figures). Let me build the data + app.

Validating app functionality and data integrity

Data's ready (2.37MB, all 23,898 real SKUs). Now the app. Let me write the explorer as a single self-contained HTML file with a virtualized table so 24K rows stay fast.

Parts Master Explorer — single-file interactive app (template with data placeholder)

Now inject the data and produce the final file.

Validating logic and scrutinizing grid design

Let me verify the file is sound — valid JSON payload, no syntax errors in the script.

Refining sort direction defaults for string columns

Solid — all 23,898 rows parse, the script is clean, and sorting surfaces sensible results (a wheel-hub assembly fitting ~20M US vehicles tops the list). I spotted one edge case: clicking the "Part type" column header has no sorter. Let me fix that.

Refined sorting logic to default strings ascending alphabetically

Let me make string-column sorts default to A–Z.

Apply A-Z default for string sorts and re-validate syntax

All four references resolve and the script is clean. Let me present it.

Presented file

I turned the pipeline's headline output — the Parts Master — into a self-contained interactive explorer over all 23,898 real SKUs.

What it does:

  • Search by part number, make, or model; filter by any of the 16 part types (chips show SKU counts); sort by US/Canada/Mexico VIO, total reach, weighted fleet age, year span, or PN.
  • Each row carries an inline stacked reach bar (US/Canada/Mexico proportions) and a coverage timeline plotting its year range on a shared 1950–2026 axis, so you can scan the whole catalog comparatively.
  • Click any SKU for a drawer: per-region reach bars, total reach, weighted average fleet year, year span, and the full fitment parsed into makes → models.
  • The masthead surfaces the aggregates straight from your data: 7.7B US vehicles-in-operation reach, 652M Canada, 615M Mexico.

A few notes on the build. I focused on the Parts Master because it's the synthesis layer — it already folds in the VIO-by-SKU counts, the weighted Avg-Age output, and the make/model rollup, so it's the richest single grain (one row per SKU). The Buyers Guide FULL is a different grain (one row per fitment, ~10MB), which would suit a drill-down "show every application for this PN" view if you want that next. It's one HTML file with the data embedded, no build step or server — opens by double-click and drops straight onto Netlify, and the table is virtualized so 24K rows stay smooth.

It's built on the 2023-06-20 catalog × 2022Q4 VIO archive that was in the zip. If you point me at a fresher MonthlyUpdateProgram.py run, I can regenerate the embedded data, or wire it to load an external JSON so it refreshes without a rebuild.

Parts master explorer

You said: how do you get this up to date?

how do you get this up to date? it says: Catalog 2023-06-20

5:14 PM

Claude responded: Good question — and the short version is that "2023-06-20" isn't the app being stale, it's the app faithfully reporting the date of the only finished output th…

Diagnosed outdated catalog date and architected refresh solution

Good question — and the short version is that "2023-06-20" isn't the app being stale, it's the app faithfully reporting the date of the only finished output that was in the zip.

Let me explain where that date comes from, then give you a one-command way to refresh it.

Why it says 2023-06-20

The explorer is built from MainOutput/Archive/2023-06-20-...-PartsMaster.xlsx — the single completed Parts Master that shipped in the zip. The date is the ACES catalog date, which the pipeline stamps into the output filename ({acesDate}-{processDate}-{processTime}-PartsMaster.xlsx).

Worth noting: your MonthlyUpdateProgram.py is already pointed at newer inputs — ACESDate = '2026-06-14' and CurrentVIOLoad = '2025Q4'. But the source files those need (the ACES XML exports in MainACES/, and the AutoCare_VCdb_*.accdb / AutoCare_PCAdb_*.accdb in the project root) weren't in the zip — MainACES/ only had 21-byte ph placeholders. So there was no newer Parts Master to embed; the 2023 archive was the freshest finished artifact.

Getting it up to date — two stages

Stage 1 — regenerate the data (on your machine, where the real inputs live):

  • Drop the current AutoCare AutoCare_VCdb_*.accdb and AutoCare_PCAdb_*.accdb in the project root.
  • Put the latest ACES XML exports in MainACES/ and the modified Experian VIO load CSVs in MainVIO/{quarter}/ (per the header rules in the README).
  • Set ACESDate / CurrentVIOLoad in MonthlyUpdateProgram.py and run it → a fresh ...-PartsMaster.xlsx lands in MainOutput/.

Stage 2 — push that into the app. This is the part I can hand you. Rather than re-embedding by hand each month, here's a script that takes any PartsMaster.xlsx and rewrites the explorer in place — keeping it a single double-clickable file, no CORS/server gotchas:

One-command updater: PartsMaster.xlsx -> refreshed self-contained explorer HTML

Let me prove it works by running it against the archived xlsx — it should parse the date from the filename and rebuild cleanly.

Verified updater functionality and prepared implementation explanation

Works end to end — it pulled 2023-06-20 from the filename, rebuilt all 23,898 rows, and the output stayed valid.

Presented file

So your monthly refresh becomes one line. Keep update_explorer.py next to parts-master-explorer.html, and after a pipeline run:

python update_explorer.py MainOutput/2026-06-14-...-PartsMaster.xlsx --vio 2025Q4

The masthead date updates to 2026-06-14, every row and aggregate is rebuilt, and the file stays a single self-contained HTML you can double-click or redeploy to Netlify. The --vio label is the one thing the xlsx doesn't carry, so pass it (it otherwise reuses whatever's already in the file). It reads columns by header name, so it won't break if column order shifts.

Two things worth flagging:

The freshness ceiling is your inputs, not the app. The Parts Master is only as current as the VCdb/PCAdb Access databases and the ACES export feeding it. If the VCdb is a few months old, new base-vehicle and engine IDs in the latest ACES won't resolve to year/make/model and those rows show as "Unknown" — so updating the AutoCare databases in the project root is the highest-leverage part of staying current.

If you'd rather not rebuild the HTML each month, the alternative is to split the data into an external data.json that the app fetches at load — then a refresh is just replacing that one file on Netlify. The tradeoff is it stops working by plain double-click (browsers block fetch over file://), so you'd need python -m http.server for local viewing. Say the word and I'll wire up that version plus a converter that emits data.json directly.

Update explorer
PY 

You said: are thes MPA PNs unviresal or for MPA only

are thes MPA PNs unviresal or for MPA only

5:16 PM

Claude responded: They're MPA-only — brand-specific SKUs, not universal interchange numbers.

Examined ACES data structure and automotive part numbering conventions

They're MPA-only — brand-specific SKUs, not universal interchange numbers.

The evidence is right in the data: the column is literally MPA PN, every source file is ACES_MPA_<brand> (Quality-Built, Black Series, Work Force, Pure Energy), and in an ACES file the <Part> element always carries that brand's own catalog number. There's no universal part number in the aftermarket — each manufacturer assigns its own, so 1003-0556M and BR55097 are Motorcar Parts of America's numbers and won't match a competitor's SKU for the same physical part.

The part that is universal in this dataset is the fitment, not the PN. Everything those part numbers are mapped to — BaseVehicle, EngineBase, the qualifiers, and the PartType ("Disc Brake Pad Set," etc.) — is coded against the AutoCare standard databases (VCdb, PCdb, PAdb, Qdb). That's the shared industry layer: any brand's ACES file describes vehicles using the same VCdb IDs, which is exactly why the pipeline can cross-reference MPA's applications against Experian VIO. So the explorer is "MPA part numbers, standardized vehicles."

If you ever wanted to make it cross-brand or universal, the part number isn't the bridge — you'd go through either interchange/cross-reference data (PN ↔ equivalent competitor PN) or GTIN/UPC, both of which are separate lookups layered on top of the ACES <Part> value. The VCdb fitment, by contrast, is already the common denominator.

can you show me the full site - and can remove the MPA PN and how is this usefull for general everyday people interested in auto parts?

5:18 PM

Claude responded: Good question to push on — and the honest answer shapes what I should build.

Synthesizing buyers guide data with parts master specifications

Good question to push on — and the honest answer shapes what I should build. Let me look at one more file in the bundle first, because it changes what's possible for a consumer-facing version.

Architecting vehicle-centric interface for everyday consumers

226,660 fitment rows with accurate per-model year ranges — this is the right backbone for a consumer tool. It even has engine detail. The part category isn't in this file, so I'll join it from the Parts Master by part number. Let me build the vehicle-centric dataset.

Describing site features and articulating everyday utility

This is the unlock. Pivoting to vehicle-first gives a clean, accurate, genuinely consumer-useful dataset: 3,185 real make/model pairs across 122 makes, each with the part categories that are covered and the exact model years — and it's tiny (0.39MB). No part numbers anywhere. Let me build the consumer edition.

Validated app functionality and strategized presenting consumer-friendly version

Built and validated — a Honda Civic, for instance, surfaces 13 covered categories with real year ranges (brake pads 1973–2022, alternator 1973–2022, wheel hub kit 1996–2010, and so on).

Presented file

Here's the full site — open whats-it-fit.html and it'll render the whole thing.

What you'll see: a "What fits your ride?" search at the top, a row of popular-make chips (Mercedes-Benz, Chevrolet, Ford, Honda…), and three category-family toggles (Braking / Electrical / Bearings & hubs). Search or pick a make, click any vehicle, and the right panel breaks down every covered part category with its exact model-year range plotted on a 1950–2026 timeline. No part numbers anywhere — I rebuilt it from the 226K-row Buyers Guide so the year ranges are accurate per make/model, then dropped the SKU layer entirely. 3,185 vehicles, 122 makes, and it's only 0.4MB so it's instant.

On the real question — is this useful to everyday people?

Honestly, the original tool mostly wasn't, for a consumer. Its headline metric, VIO (vehicles-in-operation), is a manufacturer's planning number — "how many of these vehicles are on the road, so how many parts should we make and stock." A person shopping for brake pads doesn't care that their application fits 217 million vehicles. So I deliberately cut VIO from the consumer version; keeping it would've been impressive-looking and useless.

What is useful, and what this version is built around, is the fitment knowledge buried in the catalog. For a regular person it answers a real pre-shopping question: does a replacement part for this category exist for my car, and across which years? If you've got a 2003 Civic, you can confirm in two clicks that pads, rotors, calipers, a starter, an alternator, wheel bearings, a master cylinder, and a booster are all covered — and see that, say, the wheel-hub repair kit only spans 1996–2010, which is a genuine "will this fit my year" signal.

Where it's honestly limited, and I put this right in the "About this data" panel: it's not a price guide, not a store, and it stops at category + years. It won't give a specific part number, brand, cost, or confirm your exact trim/engine — you'd verify that with a retailer. The data is also a snapshot of one catalog's coverage (mostly brake and rotating-electrical parts), not the entire parts universe, so "not covered here" doesn't mean "doesn't exist anywhere."

So the everyday value is narrow but real: a fast, browsable fitment and coverage reference — "what categories of replacement parts exist for my vehicle and which years they fit" — rather than a market-intelligence dashboard. If you wanted to push it further toward consumers, the natural next steps would be adding a Year input that filters to "parts that fit my exact year," or layering in engine/trim from the Buyers Guide so it can disambiguate the 1.3L vs 1.6L kind of split. Either is a small addition on this same data.

Architecting year-input filtering for vehicle categories

On it — adding both: an exact-year filter and engine/trim breakdown from the Buyers Guide. First I need a finer-grained dataset (per make/model/category, split by engine with year spans). Let me rebuild the data.

Architected year-filtering system with engine variant breakdown

1.54MB, engine-aware. Civic brake pads now split cleanly: All engines 1973–2022, L4·1.5L, L4·1.3L (1982–2005), L4·1.6L (1992–2000), and so on. Now the app — adding the year filter and engine breakdown.

Validated automotive compatibility across multiple model years

Everything checks out. A 1998 Civic returns 12 of 13 categories, and brake pads correctly narrow to "All engines" + the L4·1.6L (1992–2000) variant; a nonsense year like 1969 returns nothing.

Presented file

Done — both additions are live in whats-it-fit.html.

Year filter. There's now a Year box next to the search. Type, say, 1998, and the whole tool narrows to vehicles that actually have catalogued parts for that model year — the count line updates ("…with parts for 1998"), each row shows how many categories fit it, and a vehicle with no coverage for that year drops out entirely. Clear it to go back to full ranges.

Engine/trim breakdown. Every category in the detail panel now expands into its engine variants, each with its own year span and application count — so a Civic's brake pads show "All engines 1973–2022," "L4·1.6L 1992–2000," "L4·1.5L," and so on rather than one blurred range. When a year is set, the variants that fit it get pulled to the top, outlined in gold, and a thin gold marker drops on the timeline at your year; categories that don't cover the year sink to the bottom and dim out. Engine shows as "All engines" wherever the catalog didn't split by engine — I kept that honest rather than inventing detail.

A couple of notes on the build. The year coverage is computed from real merged year spans per engine variant, so it correctly handles parts that fit two separate generations with a gap. It's 1.56MB now (up from 0.4) because of the engine granularity, still a single instant-loading file. And the "About this data" panel got the matching caveat — this confirms category, engine, and years for your exact vehicle, but still isn't a price, brand, or buy-it link; verify final fitment with a retailer.

If you want to take it one more step toward a real consumer product, the obvious next move is making each result link out to a parts retailer search prefilled with the year/make/model/category — that's the bridge from "does it exist" to "here's where to get it," and it's a small addition on this same data.

Done. Each category in the detail panel now has a Shop row — Google, Amazon, eBay Motors — that opens a search prefilled for that exact vehicle and part.

The query gets sharper as you give it more. With no year, a Civic's pads search "Honda Civic brake pads." Add 1998 and it becomes "1998 Honda Civic 1.6L brake pads" — the engine size auto-appends whenever your year narrows to a single displacement, which is exactly the detail that gets you to the right part instead of a generic list. Links open in a new tab.

I kept it honest, since that was your original worry about usefulness: the links are framed as starting points, not confirmed-fit results or endorsements, both in the per-vehicle footnote and the "About this data" panel. This tool now takes a regular person the whole way — does a part for my car exist → which engine and years → here's a search to go buy it — without ever pretending to be a fitment guarantee or a store.

One build note worth flagging: I used the three retailers whose search URLs are stable, plain-text query patterns (Amazon ?k=, eBay ?_nkw=, Google ?q=), so the links won't rot. I deliberately left out catalog sites like RockAuto or AutoZone — their search routing relies on internal vehicle IDs rather than a free-text query, so a prefilled link there breaks easily. If you'd want one of those specifically, the reliable way is to map make/model/year to their catalog IDs, which is a bigger data job I can scope out if it's worth it to you.

You said: what else can I add?

what else can I add? a brand locator for the best brand?

6:21 PM

Claude responded: Quick honest answer on the "best brand" idea first, because it's the one thing I'd steer you away from: this data can't support it, and faking it would be wors…

Weighed data limitations against feature feasibility honestly

Quick honest answer on the "best brand" idea first, because it's the one thing I'd steer you away from: this data can't support it, and faking it would be worse than not having it. The whole dataset is one manufacturer's catalog (MPA's own lines — Quality-Built, Black Series, Work Force, Pure Energy). There are no competitor brands in it, no reviews, no failure rates, no warranty or price data — nothing that ranks quality. A "best brand" feature would mean inventing a ranking, which is exactly the kind of confident-but-baseless recommendation that erodes trust in an otherwise honest tool.

There's a truthful cousin of that idea, though. For some categories the catalog records the spec/material — brake pads come as Ceramic vs Semi-Metallic, for instance — and that's a real, useful "which option" distinction (ceramic = quieter and cleaner; semi-metallic = more bite and heat tolerance for towing/heavy use). That's in the Buyers Guide's Comments field, which I dropped when I built the consumer data. I could pull it back so a category shows its available materials with a plain-language "how to choose" note. That's honest because it's describing options, not declaring a winner.

Other additions that are genuinely buildable from what's already here, roughly in order of how useful I think they'd be:

  • Maintenance-job grouping — bundle related categories so the tool answers "I'm doing a brake job, what fits?" (pads + rotors + calipers + master cylinder, all on one screen with their shared year coverage). Maps cleanly to how people actually shop.
  • Shareable vehicle link — encode make/model/year in the URL so someone can bookmark or send "here's what fits my car." Tiny change, makes it feel like a real product.
  • A generic "when to replace / what to know" note per category — clearly labeled as general guidance, not from this data. Helpful for non-car-people, as long as it's honest about being generic.

The material/spec one is probably the closest to what you were reaching for with "brand," and it's the most distinctive. Want me to build that — or the maintenance-job view? I can do either.

Architected dual features: material specs and maintenance-job grouping

On it. The "best brand" can't be honest from this data, but the material/spec version can — and the catalog does record it in the Comments field I'd set aside. Let me first see exactly what spec tokens are actually in there before I build, so I extract real options and not noise.

Architected consumer-friendly product specifications and job-based filtering system

Clear signal in the data. The clean, decision-relevant options worth surfacing (with honest guidance) are: brake pads Ceramic vs Semi-Metallic, calipers Metal vs Phenolic piston, starters Automatic vs Manual transmission, and master cylinders Reservoir included or not. The rest is noise (finish disclaimers, submodel tags) I'll leave out. Let me rebuild the data with those options, then add job grouping + materials to the app.

Architected UI restructuring with job grouping and guidance toggles

All four option types extracted cleanly (Civic: ceramic/semi-metallic pads, reservoir included/not, manual/automatic starter). Now I'll rework the detail panel to group categories by repair job and show those options with plain-language guidance. Let me set up the new template.

Ran a command, viewed a file, edited a file

Now add the JOBS map, OPTGUIDE, the OPTS reference, and the how-to toggle handler. Let me insert these near the other JS constants.

Edited 2 files

Now the CSS for job headers and option chips:

Edited a file, ran a command

All 16 categories map to a job, the 8 option labels are in place, and the script is clean. Done.

Presented file

Both are in whats-it-fit.html now.

Maintenance-job grouping. Pick a vehicle and the detail panel organizes its categories under repair-job headers — "Brake job — pads, rotors, calipers," "Brake hydraulics," "Charging & starting," "Wheel bearings & hubs," and so on — each with a count. So "I'm doing a brake job, what fits?" is answered at a glance: the brake pieces sit together instead of scattered through a flat list. With a year set, the headers show how many in that job fit your year, and covered items float to the top of each group.

Material / spec options. Where the catalog actually records a choice, the category now shows option chips with a "which?" link that expands plain-language guidance:

  • Brake pads — Ceramic vs Semi-Metallic (quiet/clean daily vs more bite and heat tolerance for towing)
  • Calipers — Metal vs Phenolic piston (rugged traditional vs heat-insulating)
  • Starter — automatic vs manual transmission (match yours)
  • Master cylinder — reservoir included vs not (ready-to-bolt vs reuse/buy separately)

This is the honest version of your "best brand" instinct: it describes the real options the catalog distinguishes and helps someone choose, without inventing a ranking the data can't support. I deliberately filtered out the noise — finish disclaimers, submodel tags, torque-spec notes — so only decision-relevant specs show.

That rounds the consumer tool out to a genuine path: find your vehicle → see it grouped by the job you're doing → pick the right material/option for your needs → search to buy it — with the guardrails kept visible throughout. The open threads if you ever return are still the retailer catalog-ID mapping (for cleaner-than-search shop links) and a shareable per-vehicle URL.
